content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 12, 2017

Jason S. Rosenberg
City Attorney of South San Francisco
Meyers Nave
555 12th Street, Suite 1500
Oakland, CA 94607

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-209

Dear Mr. Rosenberg:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also, please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does Vice Mayor Liza Normandy have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act that precludes her from participating in decisions involving the application by Oyster Point Development, LLC ("OPD") based on her position as Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") for the South San Francisco Chamber of Commerce ("Chamber")?

CONCLUSION

No. Vice Mayor Normandy does not have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act.

FACTS

You are the city attorney for the City of South San Francisco. Vice Mayor Normandy serves as the CEO for the Chamber of Commerce, a position for which she receives a salary greater than $500 per year. The Chamber is a non-profit organization. OPD is a developer engaged in a significant development project within the City. OPD is also a donor to the Chamber.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-209
Page No. 2

On January 25, 2017, OPD joined the Chamber by paying the standard $300 membership.
On April 12, 2017, OPD upgraded its membership to become part of the "Chairman's Circle." The
Chairman's Circle includes three levels of membership with corresponding dues of $2,500, $5,000,
and $11,000. OPD contributed $10,000 to become one of six Chairman's Circle members.

The Chamber receives donations and membership dues each year. For the 2016-2017 fiscal
year, the Chamber received approximately $32,000 in donations from its members for its
scholarship fund. Also, the Chamber received approximately $110,938 in membership dues for the
2016 calendar year. The Chamber declared $211,721 in gross income on its 2016 tax return. Its total
budget for the 2017 calendar year is approximately $261,000.

In 2011, OPD's predecessor-in-interest received approval from the City for the development
of approximately 2.25 million square feet of research and development space in the Oyster Point
area located east of Highway 101 and west of the San Francisco Bay shoreline in South San
Francisco. Development is occurring in multiple phases, and development of the initial phases is
anticipated to begin shortly.

OPD submitted a formal planning application with the City, which will likely come before
the City Council. OPD's current application before the City seeks modifications to subsequent
phases of the development. In order to pursue the proposed new phases of development, OPD will
need to obtain a general plan amendment, zoning text amendment, and an amendment to the
development agreement between the City and OPD. Nothing in these approvals or the proposed
development involves any contribution to, or connection with, the Chamber.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100:

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section.

Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. Most pertinent
to the analysis is the following:

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating $500 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to, the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

Section 87103 also indicates that an official has a financial interest in his or her personal
finances, including of "his or her immediate family," also known as the "personal financial effects"
rule.

File No. A-17-209
Page No. 3

Foreseeability:

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard for foreseeability
differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Financial interests
that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named party in, or the subject of, a
government decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) Under the facts you have provided, the Chamber will
not be explicitly involved in any OPD-related decisions that come before the City Council. Thus,
Regulation 18701(b) provides: "[i]n general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable."2

With respect to personal finances, you have not provided any facts suggesting that the Vice
Mayor's salary will be impacted by the decisions in question. Thus, we do not further analyze
possible effects on the Vice Mayor's personal finances.

Materiality Standard – Financial Interest in a Source of Income:

Regulation 18702.3 provides the standard for materiality of a source of income. (Regulation
18702(a)(3).) The regulation indicates that an official's financial interest experiences a material
effect when the source of the income is a nonprofit that will receive a measurable financial benefit
or loss as a consequence of the decision at issue. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(3).

Under the facts that you have provided, the proposed development that OPD seeks will not
have any bearing on OPD's contribution to the Chamber. Because there is no apparent connection
between the pending application amendments and a financial effect on the Chamber, it is unlikely
that the Chamber will not receive a measurable financial benefit or loss by virtue of one of its
members developing in the City. Accordingly, the financial effect of Vice Mayor Normandy's
potential OPD-related decisions, if any, is not material.

Nexus:

Regulation 18702.3(c) sets forth the nexus test, an alternative materiality standard
potentially applicable to an official's source-of-income interest. The rationale for the nexus test is
that when an employee earns a salary to accomplish a purpose that may be advanced by what he or
she does as an official, we presume that the private employer is benefiting from the actions of the
employee in his or her official capacity. (See Torres Advice Letter, No. 17-117; Todorov Advice
Letter, No. 16-020; Yarnell Advice Letter, No. A-00-161.) However, the applicability of the nexus
test has always been premised on the fact that the decision will have a financial effect on the source.
(See Todorov Advice Letter, No. 16-020; Maltbie Advice Letter, No. A-15-243; Hooper Advice
Letter, No. 1-11-100.) Therefore, the nexus test would not be applicable to a decision's financial
effect on a member of the Chamber unless that decision would also have a financial effect on the
Chamber.

_________________________

2 Regulation 18701(b) also provides a non-exhaustive list of six factors that may be considered in the
"reasonably foreseeable" determination.

File No. A-17-209
Page No. 4

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
General Counsel

By: Ryan P. O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

RPOC:jgl

sections.question:
Does Vice Mayor Liza Normandy have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act that 
precludes her from participating in decisions involving the application by Oyster Point 
Development, LLC ("OPD") based on her position as Chief Executive Officer (''CEO") for the 
South San Francisco Chamber of Commerce ("Chamber")?

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Vice Mayor Liza Normandy have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act that 
precludes her from participating in decisions involving the application by Oyster Point 
Development, LLC ("OPD") based on her position as Chief Executive Officer (''CEO") for the 
South San Francisco Chamber of Commerce ("Chamber")?

CONCLUSION: No. Vice Mayor Normandy does not have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act.
